Notice Of Motion To Compel Further Responses To Defendant Ing'S Request For Production, Set Two
Off Calendar Mar-24-2025 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

Hearing Re: DEFENDANT DONNA ING's Motion To Compel Further Responses To Defendant Ing's Request For Production, Set Two.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendant Donna Ing brings this motion to compel further responses to her Requests for Production, Set Two, Nos. 18-22, to plaintiff Francis McGovern. For the reasons stated herein, the court denies the motion and does not award sanctions.

McGovern's complaint claims lost earnings, loss of opportunity, and loss of earning capacity arising from an accident that occurred on or about April 23, 2021. (Posner Dec., Ex. A, pp. 3, 11-12.) In her first set of RFPs, Ing asked for documents supporting these claims of loss. (Posner Dec., Ex. B, RFP Nos. 15-17.) McGovern provided documents, including invoices for his plumbing business and documents showing purported amounts he paid to others to do labor he could not perform. (Posner Dec., Ex. D.) These responses were provided on May 20, 2024. (Posner Dec., para. 7, & Ex. D.) One of the documents produced was a 2020 tax document. The present motion does not concern these RFPs or responses. In deposition on October 16, 2024, McGovern was unable to answer questions about the significance of these documents and how they were prepared, a problem compounded by his attorney's instruction to him not to answer some questions and his attorney's obtrusive speaking objections. (Posner Dec., Ex. F.) Ing propounded a second set of RFPs; these requests seek documents used to prepare McGovern's business income tax returns for 2019 through 2023. McGovern objected and did not produce responsive documents.

McGovern contends that Ing may not use a second set of RFPs to obtain documents that were covered by the first set of RFPs. The court overrules this objection. Discovery is sometimes iterative. Certainly after McGovern could not or would not answer questions about the documents he produced, and thus Ing could not understand how they were prepared, what they signified, and whether there was additional supporting documentation that would help her understand the basis for McGovern's claim of lost earnings and earning capacity, Ing was entitled to propound additional discovery on these topics.

But the court agrees with McGovern that Ing has not made a showing here to overcome the tax return privilege recognized in Webb v. Standard Oil (1957) 49 Cal.2d 509 and the cases that follow, which narrowly construe exceptions to that privilege. (See Schnabel v. Superior Court (1993) 5 Cal.4th 704, 721 ["Only one case has found that public policy mandated an exception to the privilege."].) Documents McGovern used to prepare his business taxes would likely show his business's earnings and expenditures for the years in question. But there is a more direct and less intrusive method to obtain that information: Ing can ask for business records showing McGovern's business's earnings and expenses for 2019 through 2023. She can also depose a person most qualified about those records.

The court denies the motion to compel. CCP 2031.300(c) provides that "the court shall impose a monetary sanction . . . against any party, person, or attorney who unsuccessfully makes or opposes a motion to compel a response to a demand for inspection . . . unless it finds that the one subject to the sanction acted with substantial justification or that other circumstances make the imposition of the sanction unjust." Here, the court does not award sanctions because it finds that both parties' positions respecting this motion lack substantial justification.

Order submitted.

Judge: Christine Van Aken; Clerk: V. Da Fonseca; Not reported. (301/CVA)
